comments:
Summary
The paper describes MR-SAT, a Hadoop/MapReduce implementation for tweet-level sentiment classification that (1) uses hashtags and emoticons as distant supervision labels, (2) builds feature vectors composed of words, n-grams, patterns and punctuation features (following prior work), (3) adapts an existing AkNN MapReduce classifier to perform kNN classification in a distributed fashion, and (4) encodes features via Bloom filters to reduce storage and I/O. The authors present pseudo-code for four MapReduce jobs, experiments on two Twitter datasets (hashtags and emoticons), and measurements of classification f-score, space compression, runtime and scalability on a 4-node cluster.

Strengths
- The system is implemented end-to-end in Hadoop and the paper gives clear algorithmic/pipeline descriptions and pseudo-code for the four MapReduce jobs. This makes the engineering contribution reproducible.
- Use of Bloom filters to compress intermediate feature representations and measurement of their effect on space and runtime is a useful systems-oriented contribution.
- Experiments cover multiple aspects: classification performance (multi-class and binary), effect of Bloom filters, effect of k, space compression, runtime and scalability (data size). Large datasets (hundreds of thousands to >1M tweets) are used and 10-fold cross-validation is applied.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Novelty is limited. The approach mainly adapts existing components: distant supervision via hashtags/emoticons and the features and pattern extraction of Davidov et al., together with an existing AkNN MapReduce classifier. The paper's main novelty is the integration of these parts in Hadoop and the Bloom filter encoding; this is largely an engineering/system integration effort rather than a new algorithmic advance.
- Experimental comparison to meaningful baselines is inadequate. The paper reports harmonic f-scores but does not compare against standard centralised classifiers (e.g., SVM/Naive Bayes trained on same distant-supervision labels) or against other distributed sentiment approaches (e.g., the cited [11]) or the original Davidov et al. results. Without such comparisons it is hard to judge whether MR-SAT is competitive in classification quality or offers a tradeoff worth the complexity.
- Limited error analysis and evaluation detail. The paper lacks per-class results for multi-class experiments (only aggregate scores shown), confusion matrices, or qualitative/error examples. There is also little analysis of the impact of Bloom filter collisions on classification errors (theoretical/empirical false positive rate is not reported). Choice of Bloom filter parameters (999 bits, 3 hashes) and the weight threshold w=0.005 are given without justification or sensitivity analysis.
- Cluster and scale limitations. The cluster used is small (4 VMs) and Hadoop 1.2.1; claims about large-scale suitability would be stronger with experiments on larger clusters or comparisons to faster in-memory frameworks (Spark) that the authors mention as future work.
- Some inconsistencies in preprocessing are present: the text states both that TAG/REF/URL meta-words are not used as word features (different from [6]) and also that they were substituted during preprocessing per [6]. Clarification is needed on exactly how meta-words are treated at feature extraction time.
- Presentation issues: the manuscript contains repeated fragments and some typographical/repetition errors which make parts of the narrative harder to follow. More precise description of datasets (dates, sampling strategy) and complete hyperparameter settings would help reproducibility.

Suggestions for improvement
1) Add baseline comparisons: include standard supervised classifiers trained on same distant-supervision labels and, if possible, a prior distributed approach. Report comparable metrics (precision/recall per class and macro/micro F1) so readers can assess tradeoffs.
2) Provide ablation and sensitivity studies: vary Bloom filter size and number of hash functions and report collision rates and their effect on accuracy; justify the weight threshold w and show performance across different thresholds; provide sensitivity of results to k, perhaps with curves.
3) Include per-class performance, confusion matrices, and example error cases to illustrate where the approach succeeds or fails (e.g., ambiguous hashtags, sarcastic tweets).
4) Clarify preprocessing and feature extraction details (how are TAG/REF/URL handled when not used as word features; how are non-English tokens removed; how are duplicates/retweets treated). Release code or detailed parameter/config files to aid reproducibility.
5) If claiming scalability for big data, evaluate on a larger cluster (or report scaled experiment results) and consider comparison to Spark or other distributed frameworks to show practical advantages of the Hadoop implementation.
6) Minor: clean up repetitions and typos in the text and better motivate parameter choices.

Overall assessment
MR-SAT is a practical engineering effort integrating known sentiment features and a distributed kNN classifier with Bloom-filter-based compression. The system and experiments are of interest to practitioners implementing MapReduce-based sentiment pipelines. However, the scientific contribution is modest due to limited novelty and insufficient comparative evaluation. With more rigorous baselines, parameter sensitivity analyses and clearer presentation, the work would be more convincing. As is, the paper is a solid engineering report but needs stronger empirical grounding and clearer exposition to have higher impact.
